meta.name: US Citizenship Rules Engine
meta.description: Total-function decision tree for determining U.S. citizenship. Every path terminates in a CITIZEN or NOT_CITIZEN outcome. Each node carries the controlling legal authority.
meta.disclaimer: Educational tool. Not legal advice. Citizenship determinations are fact-specific; consult a qualified immigration attorney.
legal_frontiers: The 14th Amendment 'subject to the jurisdiction' branch (node Q1) is the subject of Trump v. Barbara (cert. granted Dec 2025). Reflects settled law (Wong Kim Ark, Plyler v. Doe) as of as_of_date.

outcome_kinds.NOT_CITIZEN: Person is not a U.S. citizen (includes U.S. nationals, who are not citizens).

node_kinds.question: A node requiring an input answer; has typed 'answers' that route onward.
node_kinds.outcome: A terminal node with an 'outcome' of CITIZEN or NOT_CITIZEN.

Q0.explanation: Threshold routing question. U.S. citizenship can arise by birth in U.S. territory, birth abroad to a citizen parent, naturalization, or derivation through a parent.
Q0.citation: 14th Amendment; 8 U.S.C. § 1401; United States v. Wong Kim Ark, 169 U.S. 649 (1898)

answers.label: In a U.S. territory or possession
Abroad, both parents were U.S. citizens
Abroad, one parent was a U.S. citizen
Abroad, neither parent a citizen (or unknown)
Found in the U.S., parentage unknown

Q1.prompt: At the time of birth, was at least one parent NOT a foreign diplomat with full diplomatic immunity?
Q1.explanation: The 14th Amendment 'subject to the jurisdiction' clause excludes only children of diplomats with full immunity. It does NOT exclude children of undocumented immigrants, tourists, visa holders, or LPRs.
Q1.citation: 14th Amendment; 8 U.S.C. § 1401(a); 7 FAM 1110; Wong Kim Ark; Plyler v. Doe, 457 U.S. 202 (1982)

answers.label: Yes (parent was not a full-immunity diplomat)

Q1b.prompt: Did a parent hold only less-than-full immunity (e.g., consular officer or administrative/technical staff)?
Q1b.explanation: Full diplomatic immunity is reserved for diplomatic agents under the Vienna Convention. Consular staff have only functional immunity, which does not break jurisdiction.
Q1b.citation: 7 FAM 1112.1; Wong Kim Ark, 169 U.S. at 682

Q3.prompt: Which U.S. territory or possession was the person born in?
Q3.explanation: Each territory has its own statutory rule. American Samoa and outlying possessions produce U.S. nationals, not citizens.

Q4.prompt: Was the person born in Puerto Rico on or after January 13, 1941?

Q4b.prompt: Did the pre-1941 person acquire U.S. citizen status under the Jones Act, or reside in the U.S. on or after Jan 13, 1941?

Q5.prompt: Was at least one parent a U.S. citizen at the time of the Canal Zone birth?

Q6.prompt: Has the person (born in American Samoa/Swains Island) since become a naturalized U.S. citizen?
Q6.explanation: Persons born in American Samoa are U.S. nationals but not citizens by birth. They may naturalize.
Q6.citation: 8 U.S.C. § 1408; Tuaua v. United States, 788 F.3d 300 (D.C. Cir. 2015)

Q7.prompt: Has the person (born in an outlying possession) since become a naturalized U.S. citizen?

Q8.prompt: Was the person found in the U.S. while under age 5, with unknown parentage?

Q8b.prompt: Was it shown before the person turned 21 that they were NOT born in the United States?
Q8b.explanation: The foundling citizenship presumption is rebuttable before age 21.

Q10.prompt: Were BOTH parents U.S. citizens at the time of birth?

Q11.prompt: Had at least one citizen parent ever resided in the U.S. or a possession before the birth (any duration)?

Q20.prompt: What is the person's date-of-birth era? (Controls which transmission rule applies.)
Q20.explanation: The law in force AT THE DATE OF BIRTH controls physical-presence transmission requirements. This is the central temporal fork.
Q20.citation: 7 FAM 1130 era chart; Act of 1855; Act of May 24, 1934; Nationality Act of 1940; INA 1952; IRCA 1986

Q25.prompt: For this pre-1934 birth, which parent is the U.S. citizen?
Q25.explanation: Pre-1934 law transmitted citizenship only through the father. The 1994 Technical Amendments added § 1401(h), retroactively allowing transmission through a citizen mother who had U.S. residence.

Q25f.prompt: Did the citizen father have prior U.S. residence before the birth?

Q25m.prompt: Did the citizen mother have prior U.S. residence before the birth?

Q21.prompt: Were the parents married to each other at the time of birth?

Q22.prompt: Which parent is the U.S. citizen?

Q23.prompt: Did the citizen parent meet the physical-presence transmission requirement for the applicable era?
Q23.explanation: Era standards: post-Nov 14, 1986 = 5 years total, 2 after age 14; Dec 24, 1952 - Nov 13, 1986 = 10 years total, 5 after age 14; 1934-1952 = Nationality Act of 1940 rules. Count all days physically in the U.S.

Q24ret.prompt: Was the person born before Oct 10, 1978 AND subject to a citizenship-retention requirement?
Q24ret.explanation: Pre-1978 law required the child to reside in the U.S. for 2 years between ages 14 and 28 to RETAIN citizenship. Pub. L. 95-432 repealed this prospectively (did not restore those already lost).

Q24r2.prompt: Did the person MEET the retention requirement (2 years U.S. residence between ages 14 and 28)?

Q30.prompt: For this out-of-wedlock birth, which parent is the U.S. citizen?
Q30.citation: 8 U.S.C. § 1409; Nguyen v. INS, 533 U.S. 53 (2001); Morales-Santana

Q31.prompt: Was the citizen mother physically present in the U.S. for 1 continuous year before the birth?

Q32.prompt: For the citizen father, are ALL four § 1409(a) requirements met? (1) blood relationship by clear and convincing evidence; (2) father was a citizen at birth; (3) father agreed in writing to support until age 18; (4) legitimation/written acknowledgment/adjudication before the child turned 18.

Q33.prompt: Did the citizen father meet the physical-presence requirement (5 years total, 2 after age 14)?

Q34.prompt: Were the parents married after the birth AND was the child legitimated under the law of their residence/domicile before age 18?

GO_NAT.prompt: The person did not acquire citizenship at birth. Begin the naturalization test. Is the person a Lawful Permanent Resident (green card holder)?
GO_NAT.explanation: Bridge node: all 'no acquisition at birth' paths converge here to test naturalization.

Q48.prompt: Is the person married to and living in marital union with a U.S. citizen (3-year pathway), versus the standard 5-year pathway?

Q42.prompt: Has the person met the required continuous residence as an LPR (5 years, or 3 years for the spousal pathway)?

Q43.prompt: Is continuous residence unbroken (no single absence over 1 year without an approved N-470)?

Q44.prompt: Was the person physically present in the U.S. for at least half the required period (30 months of 5 years, or 18 months of 3 years)?

Q45.prompt: Does the person have good moral character for the statutory period?

Q45b.prompt: Is the good-moral-character bar PERMANENT (murder, or an aggravated felony committed after Nov 29, 1990)?

Q46.prompt: Can the person meet the English and civics requirements, or qualify for a waiver (50/20, 55/15, or N-648 disability)?

Q47.prompt: Is the person willing to take the Oath of Allegiance (or an eligible modified oath)?

Q49.prompt: Has the person served honorably in the U.S. armed forces?
Q49.explanation: Military service is an alternate naturalization route that can waive the LPR requirement.

Q50.prompt: Was the service during a designated period of hostilities?

Q42m.prompt: Does the person hold LPR status and have at least 1 year of honorable service?

Q60br.prompt: Is the person a child of a U.S.-citizen parent (potential derivative citizenship)?

Q61.prompt: Is the child under 18, an LPR, residing in the U.S. in the legal and physical custody of the citizen parent?
Q61.explanation: All conditions of § 1431 must be met simultaneously for automatic citizenship.

answers.label: No (child abroad or not yet LPR)

Q63.prompt: If the child is abroad, did the citizen parent apply under § 1433 and was it approved?

Q70.prompt: Has the person ever performed an expatriating act under 8 U.S.C. § 1481(a)?
Q70.explanation: All U.S. citizens — regardless of how citizenship was acquired — can lose it only by voluntarily performing one of seven acts with specific intent to relinquish: foreign naturalization, foreign oath of allegiance, foreign military service, foreign government employment, formal renunciation, wartime renunciation, or treason conviction.
Q70.citation: 8 U.S.C. § 1481(a); Afroyim v. Rusk, 387 U.S. 253 (1967); Vance v. Terrazas, 444 U.S. 252 (1980)

answers.label: Foreign naturalization / oath / military / government post

Q77.prompt: Can the government prove the person performed the act with the SPECIFIC INTENT to relinquish U.S. citizenship?
Q77.explanation: Post-Afroyim/Vance, the burden is on the government, by preponderance of evidence. State Dept policy presumes retention absent an affirmative relinquishment statement.

Q78.prompt: Was the act voluntary, free of duress, with the citizenship consequences understood?

Q80reacq.prompt: Will the former citizen (who lost status for failing the retention requirement) take the oath to regain citizenship under § 1435(d)?

Q81marr.prompt: If citizenship was lost by marriage to an alien before Sept 22, 1922, does the person apply to re-acquire under § 1435?

CIT_CONF.title: U.S. Citizen — acquisition confirmed, no valid loss of citizenship
CIT_CONF.citation: 8 U.S.C. § 1401 et seq.; Afroyim v. Rusk, 387 U.S. 253 (1967); Vance v. Terrazas, 444 U.S. 252 (1980)

NO_NATL.title: Not a U.S. Citizen — U.S. national only (§ 1408)

NO_NAT.title: Not a U.S. Citizen — naturalization requirements not met

NO_PERM.title: Not a U.S. Citizen — permanently barred from naturalization

NO_LOST.title: Not a U.S. Citizen — citizenship lost (CLN)

NO_FOUND.title: Not a U.S. Citizen — foundling presumption rebutted

NO_PR.title: Not a U.S. Citizen — Puerto Rican citizen only (pre-1941)
